points: Pressure is force per unit area (P = F/A), with SI unit N/m² or pascal (Pa). Smaller area concentrates force creating higher pressure, while larger area spreads force creating lower pressure—explaining why broad bag straps are comfortable but narrow ones hurt shoulders.

Liquids exert pressure at the bottom, on sides, and in all directions within containers. Liquid pressure depends on height of the liquid column, not the mass or container shape, which is why overhead water tanks are positioned at height to ensure adequate water pressure in taps.

The atmosphere exerts pressure on all objects from all directions—atmospheric pressure over a 15 cm × 15 cm area equals the weight of a 225 kg mass. We are not crushed because internal body pressure balances external atmospheric pressure, maintaining equilibrium.

High-speed winds create low-pressure areas between objects. When strong winds blow over house roofs, the reduced pressure above the roof combined with normal pressure below creates an upward force that can blow roofs away. Opening doors and windows equalizes internal and external pressure, preventing roof damage.

Air naturally flows from high-pressure regions to low-pressure regions. Wind speed depends on the magnitude of pressure difference—larger pressure differences create faster winds, while pressure equalization stops wind flow.

Sea breeze and land breeze result from differential solar heating: during day, warm land creates low pressure over land while cool ocean has high pressure, so air flows from sea to land. At night, warm ocean creates low pressure while cool land has high pressure, so air flows from land to sea.

Storms form when warm, moist air rises from heated land, creating low pressure and continuous air circulation. As air rises and cools, water vapour condenses into clouds and rain, while strong updrafts and downdrafts cause rubbing between water droplets and ice particles.

Lightning results from charge separation in clouds: rubbing particles create static charges with positive charges accumulating in upper regions and negative charges in lower regions. When charge build-up exceeds air's insulating capacity, sudden electrical discharge occurs as lightning, with the extreme heat producing thunder.

Lightning is extremely dangerous and requires precautions: stay away from tall objects, crouch but don't lie flat, minimize ground contact, avoid metallic umbrellas, stay in vehicles, and never enter water. Lightning conductors on buildings provide safe discharge paths into the ground.

Cyclones form over warm ocean waters through a self-reinforcing cycle: warm water heats air which rises creating low pressure; more air rushes in and rises; condensation releases latent heat; further warming drives more rising and lower pressure. Earth's rotation causes the air to spin, creating the characteristic spiral system.

Cyclones have a distinct structure with the eye at the centre having lowest pressure and calm winds, surrounded by the eyewall with the strongest winds and heaviest rainfall. Once a cyclone moves over land, the energy source (warm ocean water) is cut off and it gradually weakens.

Cyclones cause massive destruction through high-speed winds, storm surge (walls of seawater up to 3-12 metres high), heavy rainfall causing river overflow and landslides, saltwater contamination of water and soil, blocked roads, and extended power outages. Protection requires staying informed of weather alerts, maintaining emergency kits, and moving to designated cyclone shelters when warnings are issued.
